claims.normalized_claim: In Maryland v. King (2013) the Supreme Court held that taking a DNA cheek swab from a person arrested on probable cause for a serious offense, as part of a routine booking procedure, is a reasonable search under the Fourth Amendment.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Kennedy, J.), holding
counterevidence: Four justices dissented (Scalia, Ginsburg, Sotomayor, Kagan); 5-4.
allowed_wording: The Court ruled police can swab the cheek of someone arrested for a serious crime.
prohibited_wording: Police can take anyone's DNA at any time.
The Court required a warrant for the swab.

claims.normalized_claim: Maryland v. King was decided on June 3, 2013 by a 5-4 vote; the majority opinion was by Justice Anthony Kennedy, and Justice Antonin Scalia wrote the dissent, joined by Justices Ginsburg, Sotomayor, and Kagan.
allowed_wording: In a 5-4 decision in 2013, Justice Kennedy wrote for the Court; Justice Scalia led the dissent, joined by three liberal justices.

claims.normalized_claim: The majority analogized DNA swabbing to fingerprinting and photographing arrestees, framing it as a legitimate booking procedure that helps identify the person in custody.
counterevidence: The dissent rejected the identification framing (see CLM-0006).
allowed_wording: The majority called it the modern version of fingerprinting — a way to identify who is in custody.

claims.normalized_claim: Alonzo King was arrested in Maryland in 2009 on assault charges for menacing a group of people with a shotgun; under the Maryland DNA Collection Act, a cheek swab was taken from him during booking.
allowed_wording: King was arrested for assault in 2009; his cheek was swabbed at booking under a state DNA law.
prohibited_wording: King was arrested in order to get his DNA.
The swab was the reason for the arrest.
claims.notes: Arrested independently for assault; swab at booking. Do NOT imply swab was the purpose of arrest.

claims.normalized_claim: King's DNA profile matched evidence from an unsolved 2003 rape, and he was subsequently charged with and convicted of that rape.
allowed_wording: His DNA matched an unsolved 2003 rape, and he was convicted of it.
claims.notes: Downstream database hit; sober handling of sexual-assault subject.

claims.normalized_claim: In dissent, Justice Scalia argued that the swab's real purpose was to investigate unsolved crimes rather than to identify the arrestee, and that the Fourth Amendment forbids suspicionless searches of a person for evidence of crime.
allowed_wording: Scalia argued the swab was really about solving cold cases, not identification, and that the Fourth Amendment bars suspicionless searches for evidence.

claims.normalized_claim: Scalia warned that, as a consequence of the decision, 'your DNA can be taken and entered into a national DNA database if you are ever arrested, rightly or wrongly, and for whatever reason,' and he read a summary of his dissent aloud from the bench.
allowed_wording: Scalia warned your DNA can be taken if you are ever arrested 'rightly or wrongly, and for whatever reason,' and read part of his dissent from the bench.
claims.notes: Quote is 'national DNA database'; bench reading is a reporting fact (SRC-0003).

claims.normalized_claim: The DNA was taken at arrest, before any conviction; many states and the federal government have DNA-collection laws, and arrestee profiles can be run through CODIS, the FBI's national DNA database.
allowed_wording: The swab happens at arrest, before conviction; profiles go into CODIS, the FBI's national DNA database.
prohibited_wording: Every state takes DNA from everyone arrested.
claims.notes: Pre-conviction; CODIS; laws vary by state.

claims.normalized_claim: The case was a genuine clash between two interests: the majority emphasized identification and public-safety benefits of DNA at booking, while the dissent emphasized the privacy intrusion of suspicionless DNA searches.
allowed_wording: It pit catching criminals against the privacy of people who have only been arrested.

findings: Durable hashes 2/3 (SRC-0002 blocked). Framing: swab not arrest's purpose. Neutral; sober.
